hypothesis: Replacing the B0-era gain-product off-diagonal covariance approximation (cov_post[i,j] ~= Phi(alpha_i)*Phi(alpha_j)*cov_pre[i,j]) with the exact bivariate ReLU cross-moment closes a meaningful fraction of the ~9x gap between covariance-propagation and the MC champion, per the lead's compounding-off-diagonal-bias theory.

evaluation.command: uv run --frozen whest run --estimator <estimator> --runner subprocess --dataset hf://aicrowd/arc-whestbench-public-2026@v1-phase1 --split mini --flop-budget 272000000000 --format json

reports.note: Champion report is byte-identical to B4's champion run (same estimator.py@1598169, same deterministic dataset/seeds) and was reused rather than re-executed; the candidate report is freshly executed for B3.

aggregate.reference_B0_covariance_propagation_note: The B3 candidate (exact cross-moment) scores 8.4606e-06 vs. the B0-era gain-product approximation's own official Mini score of 8.3663e-06 (~1% apart) -- i.e. statistically indistinguishable in this aggregate. The exact fix does not materially move covariance propagation's score in either direction; both remain ~9x worse than the MC champion.

paired.definition: candidate_adjusted_score_m - champion_adjusted_score_m, matched by mlp_name/mlp_index; champion is the current promoted champion (plain 6500-sample MC), per protocol -- not the gain-product covariance-propagation baseline this candidate is architecturally derived from

diagnostic.purpose: Before accepting the paired-gate result, verified the exact cross-moment formula and its implementation were not simply buggy, since the initial 788% regression number looked catastrophic in isolation.
diagnostic.method: 1) Validated the closed-form cross-moment integral (derived via Price's theorem + arccos-substitution quadrature) against brute-force 20M-sample Monte Carlo for 13 (mu_x,mu_y,sigma_x,sigma_y,rho) test points spanning rho up to 0.999999 and various signs -- all matched to ~1e-4. 2) Called candidate_claude.py's actual Estimator.predict() in-process (bypassing the CLI/subprocess runner) on real dataset MLP #0 and compared its final-layer output directly against examples/03_covariance_propagation.py's gain-product Estimator on the same MLP.
diagnostic.result: The two methods' final-layer outputs are nearly identical (L2 diff norm 0.0152 vs |mu|~12.4, ~0.1% relative). Against an independent 2,000,000-sample Monte Carlo reference for that MLP, the exact-cross-moment candidate was actually marginally *more* accurate than the gain-product baseline (MSE 3.524e-05 vs 4.133e-05). This confirms the formula and implementation are correct -- the exact correction genuinely has very small magnitude relative to the leading gain-product term for this network's actual (alpha, rho) statistics, so it does not meaningfully change the aggregate final-layer score either way. The paired-gate REJECTED verdict below is a real, expected result (analytic methods trailing the MC champion by ~9x), not an implementation defect.

reason: Candidate trails the current MC champion by ~9x (as any analytic covariance-propagation method starts out, per the lead's own B1 note), and the exact off-diagonal cross-moment correction does not close that gap: it scores statistically indistinguishable from (very slightly worse than) the B0-era gain-product approximation it replaces (8.4606e-06 vs 8.3663e-06). This refutes the hypothesis that compounding off-diagonal bias over 32 layers is a dominant error term for covariance propagation -- the correction is mathematically validated and correctly implemented (see diagnostic above) but practically negligible here. Remaining error is more likely dominated by something the pairwise-Gaussian cross-moment fix cannot touch: e.g. higher-order (beyond-pairwise) non-Gaussianity introduced by ReLU, or breakdown of the mean-field/Gaussian assumption itself at depth 32.
